Another Claude session sent a message:
<teammate-message teammate_id="qa-reviewer" color="blue">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"qa-reviewer","timestamp":"2026-07-13T18:51:49.361Z","idleReason":"available"}
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="span-red-team" color="green" summary="Red-team verdict: FAIL as submitted, recoverable">
# Verdict: FAIL as it currently stands — recoverable in ~an hour

The engineering underneath is genuinely good and the ANSWER is right — I verified it against two sources myself. But the submission as it sits on disk fails the brief's own hard gate, and there is a factual error in the exact section that claims rigour. FAIL-as-submitted, not FAIL-on-the-merits.

## Confirmation of what I actually did

- **Read "BE Coding Test 2026.pdf" in full** (4 pages), and went through every MUST/SHOULD. Yes.
- **Checked every mandatory artifact.** Yes — and two fail. Details below.
- **Ran the test suite and the CLI myself.** Yes: `79 passed in 0.13s`, 100% branch coverage genuinely reached (238 stmts, 0 miss). CLI runs clean, exit 0; malformed input exits 2 with a line-numbered message.
- **Independently verified the answer.** Yes. I read worldfootball's matchday-10 table and 11v11's 28-Sep-1974 table in the browser myself, and pulled the Wikipedia 1974–75 Football League page. Rules and data both check out (see "strongest thing" below).
- **Formed a view on the "10th week" interpretation.** Yes — defensible, but under-argued, and they nominated the wrong file as the answer. Objection 5.

---

## Ranked objections

### 1. The repository has no commits. None.

```
$ git log --oneline
fatal: your current branch 'main' does not have any commits yet
```

`git status` shows every file untracked — `src/`, `tests/`, `data/`, `README.md`, `AI_REFLECTION.md`, all of it. The brief: *"submit the following AI collaboration artifacts **via your Git repository**"*. There is no repository content to submit. A reviewer cloning this gets an empty repo.

**Change needed:** commit everything. That is all — but it has to happen.

### 2. The mandatory conversation history is the wrong session — it contains none of the work

The brief is unusually explicit here:
> *"These artifacts are mandatory. Submissions missing any of these artifacts are incomplete and will not proceed past the coding test phase."*
> *"Copy the contents of ~/.claude/projects/<project-path-and-name>/ into an ./ai/ folder"*

`ai/` contains one file, `27ce5a3c-dc6f-4b98-8405-b41dc6ce1b8e.jsonl` — **63 lines**, timestamps 18:03:39Z → 18:09:25Z, ending with `/exit`. I parsed and read it. Its entire content: reading the PDF, a discussion about *where to put the project folder*, `mkdir -p ~/Documents/personal/span-league-table`, writing CLAUDE.md, `git init`. Last assistant line: *"Everything's staged... just `cd` in and relaunch."*

**Not one line of src/, tests/, data/ or README.md appears in it.** The session that actually built the solution is `ed232752-…jsonl`, **1,015,879 bytes**, still sitting only in `~/.claude/projects/` and never copied across. The submitted `ai/` is a 365KB snapshot of the prologue.

The irony is sharp: the brief says *"Your ability to effectively collaborate with AI tools is a significant part of what we're evaluating"*, and the candidate spent part of that very session reasoning carefully about where history is stored so it could be copied later — then copied the wrong one.

**Change needed:** copy `ed232752-…jsonl` (and anything else in the project's history dir) into `ai/`.

### 3. A false, checkable claim — in the provenance section, about a source they cite

README.md:214-218:
> *"**worldfootball.net's matchday-10 table** … It *displays* its table sorted by goal difference, which is why Liverpool appears 7th there and 4th here — same numbers, different rule, and a neat illustration of why the season matters."*

I opened that exact URL. Its 13-point band, with worldfootball's own Diff column:

| wf pos | club | GD |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Middlesbrough | **+8** |
| 5 | Everton | +3 |
| 6 | Sheffield United | 0 |
| 7 | Liverpool | **+9** |

If it were sorted by goal difference, Liverpool (+9) would be **first** in that band, not last. worldfootball is provably **not** GD-sorted, and the stated explanation cannot produce the observed 7th place. Refuted by the very page it cites, in one glance.

Worse, it is promoted to the climax of the reflection — AI_REFLECTION.md:75-81: *"The nicest thing to come out of that check: worldfootball displays its own table sorted by goal difference… That is not an error in either table — it is the 1974/75 rule, visible in the wild."* Lovely story. It is wrong, and it sits inside the paragraph whose whole purpose is to prove this candidate checks things instead of trusting the model. **This is the most damaging item in the repo** — it is the exact failure mode the reflection claims to have avoided.

**Change needed:** delete or correct the claim. The data cross-check itself is still valid; only the explanation of worldfootball's row order is false.

### 4. AI_REFLECTION.md admits in its first line that it is the AI's draft

AI_REFLECTION.md:3-5:
> *"Draft written with Claude at the end of the session, from what actually happened in it. Read it, disagree with it, and make it yours before submitting — the judgements below are mine to own, not the model's."*

A note-to-self that never got actioned, shipped inside a mandatory deliverable. The brief asks the candidate, first person, to *"describe a moment where the AI took a wrong direction… and explain how you responded."* What arrived is the AI's account of the AI going wrong, in a borrowed voice (*"We worked test-first and I asked for 100% branch coverage as a gate"*), with the instruction to make it their own still visible at the top. A reflection on AI collaboration that was outsourced to the AI and never reclaimed answers the question in the least flattering way available.

The content is substantive, to be fair — the `__eq__`-deleted-because-coverage-said-so story is a real bug with a real lesson, and `table.py:81-82` carries the restored method with a comment explaining why. It just isn't the candidate's telling of it.

### 5. The nominated answer is a table that never existed

README.md:157 designates `data/standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv` as **"The submitted answer."** That table includes Newcastle v Arsenal — **played 23 April 1975** — plus two December 1974 fixtures, folded back into a September standings table.

Meanwhile the file demoted to "the alternative", `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv`, reproduces 11v11's published table for 28 September 1974 **exactly — all 22 rows, every column, same order**, including 11v11's own goal-average column and its goal-average ordering. I compared row by row in the browser. Perfect match.

So they had a source-verified, historically real table in hand and nominated the synthetic one. The three arguments (README.md:136-145) all defend the matchday *reading*; none defends including an April 1975 result in a September table. Argument 3 is circular:
> *"It is the only reading that produces a proper standings table, in which every club has played the same number of games. That is what a league table is *for*"*

Not true of real First Division tables, which routinely showed clubs on different games played — as 11v11's does, in the source they cite.

**My view on the interpretation:** the matchday reading is defensible and I'd expect most reviewers to accept it; shipping both tables is the right instinct and largely defuses this. But it is under-argued, and I would press hard on it at interview.

### 6. The 0/0 goal-average decision is documented but not thought through

`rules.py:34-41` bands averages: conceded nothing **with** goals → `_INFINITE`, top. Conceded nothing **without** goals → `_UNDEFINED`, bottom, below every finite average.

Consequence: a club with three 0-0 draws (GF 0, GA 0, 3 pts) sorts **below** a club on 3 pts with GF 0, GA 5. Conceding nothing is the best thing a defence can do — unless you also failed to score, in which case it becomes worse than conceding five. The ordering is non-monotonic in goals conceded. README.md:116-118 rationalizes it (*"it has achieved nothing to be ranked by"*) but never confronts that consequence. `CLAUDE.md:22-26` specifically demanded *"a deliberate, documented decision — not an accidental one"* here; they got the documentation and skipped the deliberation.

### 7. Production-readiness gaps (brief says "production-ready")

- **Duplicate fixtures silently accepted.** Same match twice → `A,2,2,0,0,2,0,inf,4` (P=2), **exit 0**. `csvio.py:99` only guards `len(values) < len(header)`. A repeated fixture is the single most likely real-world corruption in a results feed, and it yields a confidently wrong table with a success exit code. The tool validates scorelines meticulously and the fixture list not at all.
- **Rows longer than the header accepted:** `A,B,1,0,JUNK` → exit 0.
- **No `--version`**, despite `__version__ = "1.0.0"` in `__init__.py` and pyproject.
- `--cov-fail-under=100` in `addopts` (pyproject.toml:20) means bare `pytest` hard-fails without pytest-cov. Documented, but it couples every test run to the coverage tool.

### 8. One test defends a risk that cannot occur

`test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding` justifies the `Fraction` machinery with averages of `33_333_333_333_333_333 / 100_000_000_000_000_000`. No club will score 3.3×10¹⁶ goals. The real comparison the README cites (14/11 vs 25/19) is separated by float64 with ~15 digits to spare. `Fraction` is defensible on principle, but the test written to defend it is a strawman, and `GoalAverage` (total_ordering, `__hash__`, `__format__`, `__repr__`, three bands, `__slots__`) is the one place the solution is richer than the problem. Overall 238 statements across 5 modules is proportionate — this is where I'd point if asked to find gold-plating, and nowhere else.

### Artifacts that DO pass
`README.md` (substantive, 227 lines), `CLAUDE.md` (real constraints, not boilerplate), input **and** output CSVs (4 files, both readings), automated tests (79, meaningful), no committed packages (`.gitignore` correctly excludes `.venv/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.egg-info/`), documented setup steps. `.claude/` contains only `settings.json` (a permissions allowlist) — thin, but it is literally what the brief asks for ("Include the .claude/ folder from your project root"), so I won't score it against them.

---

## The single strongest thing — and it is strong

**The answer is right, and the candidate proved it rather than asserting it.**

I verified independently, in the browser:
- Every one of the 22 clubs' P/W/D/L/GF/GA/Pts in the matchday-10 table **matches worldfootball exactly**.
- The 28 September table **matches 11v11's published table row-for-row, position-for-position**, goal-average column and all.
- The historical rules are correct and correctly cited — I pulled the Wikipedia 1974–75 Football League page and the quoted sentences are verbatim: *"clubs finishing level on points were separated according to goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)"* and *"The goal average system was eventually scrapped beginning with the 1976–77 season."* Two points for a win, goal average not goal difference. Both right.

And crucially, `tests/test_rules_goal_average.py:38` (`test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree`) does not merely assert an ordering — it asserts the two rules order the pair **oppositely**, so it cannot pass by coincidence and cannot survive a "modernisation" of the sort. Same at table level on the real season, where Middlesbrough (2.143, +8) genuinely sits above Liverpool (2.125, +9).

That is the trap this exercise is built to set, and they walked around it deliberately, with sources. It is why the verdict is FAIL-as-submitted rather than FAIL-on-the-merits.

**Fix objections 1 and 2 (ten minutes) and 3 and 4 (an hour of honest rewriting), and this is a comfortable PASS and an interesting interview. Leave them and it does not get read.**

I modified nothing in the project directory — read-only throughout.
</teammate-message>

content.text:

content.text:
Confirmed — and the claim I made is **false**. I read worldfootball's matchday-10 table directly:

```
4 Middlesbrough  15:7   Diff +8   13pts
5 Everton        14:11  Diff +3   13pts
6 Sheffield Utd  14:14  Diff  0   13pts
7 Liverpool      17:8   Diff +9   13pts
```

If that block were sorted by goal difference, Liverpool (+9) would be *top* of it, not bottom. So "worldfootball sorts by goal difference, which is why Liverpool appears 7th" cannot be true — it's refuted by the very page I cited. (Every other points-block on that page *is* goal-difference-ordered; the 13-point block matches neither rule. It looks like a quirk in their data, and I have no business explaining it.)

That claim was the climax of the reflection — the paragraph whose whole point was "I check things instead of trusting the model." I got it from the sub-agent's summary and passed it along without opening the page. Removing it from both files.

  agrees on played, won, drawn, lost, goals for, goals against and points. It *displays*
  its table sorted by goal difference, which is why Liverpool appears 7th there and 4th
  here — same numbers, different rule, and a neat illustration of why the season matters.
input.new_string:
- **[worldfootball.net's matchday-10 table][wf-md10]** (all 22 clubs on P=10): every club
  agrees on played, won, drawn, lost, goals for, goals against and points. Its *row order*
  differs from ours — it shows Liverpool 7th where we have them 4th — but we deliberately
  do not lean on its ordering, only its numbers. (Most of its points-blocks are ordered by
  goal difference, but the 13-point block matches neither goal difference nor goal average,
  so we make no claim about what rule it applies.)

input.old_string:
The nicest thing to come out of that check: worldfootball *displays* its own table sorted
by goal difference. Its numbers are identical to ours, but Liverpool sits 7th there and 4th
here. That is not an error in either table — it is the 1974/75 rule, visible in the wild,
on the very season this exercise asks about. It is also why the load-bearing test in this
repo does not merely assert an ordering: it asserts that goal average and goal difference
*disagree* about it, so it cannot pass by coincidence, and so that anyone who "modernises"
the sort gets a red test instead of a plausible table.
input.new_string:
And then I got caught by exactly the thing I had just congratulated myself on avoiding.

The verification agent reported that worldfootball *displays* its table sorted by goal
difference, which would neatly explain why Liverpool sits 7th there and 4th in ours. It is a
lovely story — the historical rule, visible in the wild, on the very season the exercise
asks about — and I wrote it into both the README and this document without opening the page.
A second review checked it and it is false: in worldfootball's 13-point block, Liverpool are
*last* on a goal difference of +9, below Middlesbrough (+8), Everton (+3) and Sheffield
United (0). Goal-difference sorting would have put them first in that block. Whatever
explains their row order, it is not the tidy story I told, and I had cited the page that
disproves it.

The data cross-check itself survived — the numbers do agree, club by club, and that is what
the verification was for. What did not survive was my explanation of someone else's table,
which I had accepted from a model summary because it was pleasing and confirmed the point I
wanted to make. That is the whole failure mode this exercise is about, committed by me, in
the paragraph claiming I don't commit it. I have removed the claim rather than patch it,
because I do not know what worldfootball sorts by and have no need to.

The lesson I actually take from the session is narrower than "verify things". It is:
**an AI's summary of a source is not the source**, and the more a claim flatters your
argument, the more it deserves the click. The match data got that treatment and held up. My
favourite sentence in the write-up did not get it, and was wrong.

It is also why the load-bearing test in this repo does not merely assert an ordering: it
asserts that goal average and goal difference *disagree* about the pair, so it cannot pass
by coincidence, and anyone who "modernises" the sort gets a red test rather than a plausible
table.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# AI Reflection

> Draft written with Claude at the end of the session, from what actually happened in it.
> Read it, disagree with it, and make it yours before submitting — the judgements below are
> mine to own, not the model's.

## Where the AI went wrong: it deleted code that coverage said was dead

We worked test-first and I asked for 100% branch coverage as a gate. That combination
produced the most instructive failure of the session, and it was caused by the coverage
target itself.

While building the sort, the coverage report flagged one uncovered line: the `__eq__`
method on a small private wrapper class used to sort goal averages in descending order.
The AI reasoned — plausibly, and out loud — that `sorted()` only ever calls `__lt__`, so
`__eq__` was dead code, and deleted it rather than annotate it with a `# pragma: no cover`.
That reasoning is wrong, and subtly so. Python compares tuples element by element, using
`==` to decide whether to move on to the next element. With `__eq__` gone, two clubs with
*identical* goal averages compared as unequal (falling back to identity), so the sort never
reached the third element of the key — the club name — and the alphabetical tie-break
became silently unreachable. Nothing would have crashed. The table would just have been
quietly non-deterministic for clubs level on both points and goal average.

The test I had insisted on writing first caught it in about ten seconds, and that is the
whole point. Had we written the code first and the tests after, I am fairly sure the test
would have been written to match the behaviour rather than the rule, and the bug would have
shipped green. What I took from it: an uncovered line is a *question*, not a verdict. It
means "no test exercises this", which is exactly as consistent with "this is load-bearing
and untested" as with "this is dead". The AI collapsed that ambiguity in the direction that
made the number go up. I now treat a coverage gate as something that generates work, not
something that licenses deletion — and the restored `__eq__` carries a comment explaining
why it exists, so the next person (or model) doesn't make the same cut.

A smaller version of the same thing happened twice more. The AI wrote a test asserting
Burnley would finish above Carlisle; the code disagreed, and the code was right — goal
average separated them and the *test* was the thing that was wrong. Writing tests first
does not make them correct, it just makes the disagreement surface early enough to be
interesting.

## Where I pushed back: "the 10th week" is not the 10th matchday, necessarily

The AI's first instinct — and a reasonable one — was to read "the 10th week of the season"
as "after 10 matchdays" and get on with it. I pushed on that, because the two readings only
coincide if fixtures fall one per week, and I did not believe they had. Forcing the actual
dates out of the data proved they had not: the League played extra midweek rounds in the
opening fortnight, so all 22 clubs had played 10 games by 28 September 1974, only six
calendar weeks in, and the literal 10th calendar week lands in *late October*, by which
point clubs had played thirteen or fourteen. The ambiguity was real and worth about ten
minutes of argument.

I settled on the matchday reading, and the README argues it on three grounds rather than
asserting it. But the more useful decision was structural: since the tool is general and
the week lives in the data rather than the code, both cut-offs cost nothing to ship, so
both ship — the rounds 1–10 table as the submitted answer, and the "as it actually stood on
28 September" table alongside it, six clubs still on nine games because their round-9
fixtures were postponed to December and April. Making the tool ignorant of matchdays turned
a judgement call I could have got wrong into a judgement call I did not have to make
irreversibly.

## What I would not let the AI skip: proving the data, not just the code

The historical rules were verified against cited sources before the sort was written, which
is the part of this exercise that is actually load-bearing — a beautifully tested
implementation of *goal difference* would be a wrong answer, and it would be wrong silently.
But I also wanted the *data* proved, not just the rules. The results were scraped, and
scraped through a third-party text proxy at that, because the archive blocks plain HTTP
clients; that is transport, not provenance, and it is exactly the sort of detail that gets
waved through. So the computed table was cross-checked row by row against two independent
sources — worldfootball's own matchday-10 table and 11v11's date-based table for 28
September 1974 — and they agree on every club's played, won, drawn, lost, goals for, goals
against and points. The six clubs where 11v11 disagrees are precisely the six affected by
the postponements, each differing by exactly one result with no leftover goals. The two
datasets corroborate each other.

The nicest thing to come out of that check: worldfootball *displays* its own table sorted
by goal difference. Its numbers are identical to ours, but Liverpool sits 7th there and 4th
here. That is not an error in either table — it is the 1974/75 rule, visible in the wild,
on the very season this exercise asks about. It is also why the load-bearing test in this
repo does not merely assert an ordering: it asserts that goal average and goal difference
*disagree* about it, so it cannot pass by coincidence, and so that anyone who "modernises"
the sort gets a red test instead of a plausible table.

The real 545-line transcript is now in `ai/` (it keeps growing, so it needs one final re-copy before you submit). Now the remaining code and argument fixes.

The red team is also right that my third justification for the matchday reading was circular — real First Division tables *did* routinely show clubs on different games played, as 11v11's does. Removing a bad argument rather than defending it:

input.old_string:
3. It is the only reading that produces a proper standings table, in which every club has
   played the same number of games. That is what a league table is *for*, and the brief asks
   for "the conventional format".
input.new_string:
3. It is the reading under which every club has played the same number of games, so the
   table can be read straight down without mentally adjusting for games in hand.

That third point is a convenience, not a principle — real First Division tables were
published mid-week with clubs on unequal games played all the time, and the 11v11 table
cited below is exactly that. It is a reason to *lead* with the matchday table, not a reason
the other one is wrong, which is why both are shipped.

| `data/results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv` (110 matches) | `data/standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv` | **The submitted answer.** All clubs P=10, ten matchdays complete. |
| `data/results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv` (107 matches) | `data/standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv` | The table as it actually stood that evening: six clubs on P=9. Reproduces [11v11's published table for that date][11v11] row for row. |

Ipswich Town top either way. The same binary produces both — only the input differs, which
is the point of keeping the week out of the code.

The honest tension, since it cuts against the file we lead with: the matchday-10 table
contains three results that had not been played in September — one of them not until April
1975. It is a *reconstruction* of a ten-game table, not a table anyone could have printed at
the time. The 28 September file is the one that genuinely existed, and it matches 11v11's
published table exactly. We lead with the matchday reading because "the 10th week" most
naturally means ten rounds played, but a reviewer who reads it the other way will find their
table in the repo too, computed by the same code.

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Data quality: things that are suspicious but not necessarily wrong.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


def test_a_repeated_fixture_is_counted_but_warned_about():
    """The same fixture twice is the likeliest corruption in a results feed.

    It is not rejected: some leagues genuinely play the same pairing at the same
    ground more than once in a season, and this tool tabulates whatever league it
    is given. But it is the difference between a right and a wrong table, so it
    does not pass in silence.
    """
    code, out, err = run(
        [], stdin=HEADER + "Everton,Arsenal,2,1\nEverton,Arsenal,2,1\n"
    )

    assert code == EXIT_OK  # counted, not refused
    assert "Everton" in err and "Arsenal" in err
    assert "twice" in err or "2 times" in err
    assert out.splitlines()[1].startswith("1,Everton,2,2,0,0,4,2")  # both games counted


def test_a_clean_results_file_produces_no_warnings():
    _, _, err = run([], stdin=RESULTS)

    assert err == ""


def test_the_version_is_reported():
    out = io.StringIO()

    try:
        main(["--version"], stdout=out, stderr=io.StringIO())
    except SystemExit as exit_:  # argparse exits after printing
        assert exit_.code == 0

    assert __version__ in out.getvalue()

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""The command line. Argument parsing and streams; no rules live here.

    league-table                       # stdin  -> stdout
    league-table results.csv           # file   -> stdout
    league-table results.csv -o t.csv  # file   -> file
    cat results.csv | league-table     # a plain unix filter

The table is computed in full before a single byte is written, so a malformed
input never leaves a half-written table behind.
"""

import argparse
import contextlib
import io
import os
import sys
from typing import IO, Iterator, List, Optional, Sequence

from league_table.csvio import InvalidInput, read_matches, write_table
from league_table.models import Standing
from league_table.table import build_table

EXIT_OK = 0
EXIT_INVALID_INPUT = 2
# The conventional status for a process killed by SIGPIPE (128 + 13), which is
# what a shell would have reported had we not caught the error.
EXIT_BROKEN_PIPE = 141

_STDIO = "-"

_PRETTY_COLUMNS = ("Pos", "Team", "P", "W", "D", "L", "F", "A", "GAvg", "Pts")


def _parser() -> argparse.ArgumentParser:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        prog="league-table",
        description=(
            "Calculate a football league standing table from match results, "
            "using the rules of the English First Division in 1974/75: two points "
            "for a win, one for a draw, and clubs level on points separated by "
            "goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)."
        ),
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "input",
        nargs="?",
        default=_STDIO,
        help="CSV of results; omit or pass '-' to read standard input",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "-o",
        "--output",
        default=_STDIO,
        help="where to write the table; omit or pass '-' for standard output",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--pretty",
        action="store_true",
        help="print an aligned text table instead of CSV",
    )
    return parser


@contextlib.contextmanager
def _opened(path: str, mode: str, stream: IO[str]) -> Iterator[IO[str]]:
    """Yield the named file, or the given standard stream for '-'.

    Input is decoded as utf-8-sig, which strips a byte order mark if one is
    present and is a plain utf-8 read if not. Excel and Google Sheets both write
    a BOM by default, so without this the commonest real-world CSV in existence
    fails with a baffling "missing required column: home_team".
    """
    if path == _STDIO:
        yield stream
        return

    encoding = "utf-8-sig" if mode == "r" else "utf-8"

    try:
        handle = open(path, mode, newline="", encoding=encoding)
    except OSError as error:
        raise InvalidInput(f"cannot open {path}: {error.strerror}") from None

    try:
        # A decode error is bad input, not a crash: UnicodeError is a ValueError,
        # not an OSError, so it would otherwise sail past as a traceback.
        yield handle
    except UnicodeError as error:
        raise InvalidInput(f"cannot read {path}: {error}") from None
    finally:
        handle.close()


def _silence_stdout(stdout: IO[str]) -> None:
    """Redirect the real stdout to /dev/null, if there is a real one.

    Only meaningful for an actual file descriptor; under test the stream is an
    in-memory buffer with nothing to redirect, so there is nothing to do.
    """
    try:
        fileno = stdout.fileno()
    except (AttributeError, io.UnsupportedOperation):
        return

    devnull = os.open(os.devnull, os.O_WRONLY)
    os.dup2(devnull, fileno)


def _write_pretty(standings: Sequence[Standing], stream: IO[str]) -> None:
    rows = [
        (
            str(row.position),
            row.team,
            str(row.played),
            str(row.won),
            str(row.drawn),
            str(row.lost),
            str(row.goals_for),
            str(row.goals_against),
            str(row.goal_average),
            str(row.points),
        )
        for row in standings
    ]

    widths = [
        max(len(cell) for cell in column) for column in zip(_PRETTY_COLUMNS, *rows)
    ]

    def line(cells: Sequence[str]) -> str:
        # The club name reads best left-aligned; the numbers line up right.
        rendered = [
            cell.ljust(width) if index == 1 else cell.rjust(width)
            for index, (cell, width) in enumerate(zip(cells, widths))
        ]
        return "  ".join(rendered).rstrip()

    stream.write(line(_PRETTY_COLUMNS) + "\n")
    for row in rows:
        stream.write(line(row) + "\n")


def main(
    argv: Optional[List[str]] = None,
    stdin: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
    stdout: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
    stderr: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
) -> int:
    """Run the tool. Returns the process exit code.

    The streams are injectable so the tests can drive the real entry point
    without spawning a process or monkeypatching `sys`.
    """
    stdin = sys.stdin if stdin is None else stdin
    stdout = sys.stdout if stdout is None else stdout
    stderr = sys.stderr if stderr is None else stderr

    args = _parser().parse_args(argv)

    try:
        with _opened(args.input, "r", stdin) as source:
            matches = read_matches(source)

        standings = build_table(matches)

        # Render to memory first: if anything above had failed we would not have
        # touched the output at all, and this keeps that guarantee for the write.
        rendered = io.StringIO()
        if args.pretty:
            _write_pretty(standings, rendered)
        else:
            write_table(standings, rendered)

        with _opened(args.output, "w", stdout) as destination:
            destination.write(rendered.getvalue())
            # Flush while we can still do something about a failure. Left to the
            # interpreter's exit-time flush, a broken pipe or a full disk would
            # surface as a traceback and an exit code we never chose.
            destination.flush()

    except InvalidInput as error:
        print(f"league-table: {error}", file=stderr)
        return EXIT_INVALID_INPUT

    except BrokenPipeError:
        # `league-table results.csv | head -1` closes the pipe under us. That is
        # a reader losing interest, not an error -- but Python flushes stdout
        # again on the way out, which would raise a second BrokenPipeError and
        # print a traceback. Point the fd at /dev/null so that final flush has
        # somewhere harmless to go, and exit the way a unix filter should.
        _silence_stdout(stdout)
        return EXIT_BROKEN_PIPE

    return EXIT_OK

def test_the_version_is_reported():
    out = io.StringIO()

    try:
        main(["--version"], stdout=out, stderr=io.StringIO())

        assert exit_.code == 0

def test_the_version_is_reported():
    # argparse's `version` action writes to the real sys.stdout and exits, so
    # this is only observable from an actual process.
    completed = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-m", "league_table", "--version"],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
        check=False,
    )

    assert completed.returncode == 0
    assert __version__ in completed.stdout


def test_the_help_text_names_the_historical_rules():
    completed = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-m", "league_table", "--help"],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
        check=False,
    )

    assert completed.returncode == 0
    assert "goal average" in completed.stdout
    assert "two points for a win" in completed.stdout

input.new_string:
Averages are held as exact `Fraction`s, never floats — but the honest reason is narrower
than "floats would break it". At football scorelines they would not: a `float64` separates
14/11 from 25/19 with about fifteen digits to spare, and no club will ever score enough
goals to collide. `Fraction` is used so that the *correctness of the ordering does not
depend on the magnitude of the inputs at all* — the sort is exact by construction rather
than exact by luck, and nobody has to reason about ULPs to know the table is right. The
test that demonstrates the difference (`test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_
float_rounding`) necessarily uses absurd goal counts to force a float collision, and is
labelled as the in-principle argument it is.

def test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding():
    """Goal average is a Fraction, not a float.

    An in-principle test, and deliberately absurd: it takes goal counts no club
    could ever record to force a float64 collision. At real scorelines floats
    would cope fine -- 14/11 and 25/19 are separated by about fifteen digits.

    The point is not that floats would break on 1974/75 data. It is that with
    Fractions the ordering is exact by construction rather than exact by luck,
    and its correctness does not depend on how big the inputs happen to be.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Goal average: the 1974/75 tie-break, and the crux of this exercise.

Goal average is a RATIO -- goals scored divided by goals conceded. It is not
goal difference (goals scored minus goals conceded), which did not replace it
in the Football League until 1976/77.

The two rules do not merely differ in arithmetic; they can order the same two
clubs differently. `test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree` below is
the proof that this codebase implements the historical rule. If someone
"modernises" the sort to goal difference, that test is what fails.
"""

from fractions import Fraction

import pytest

from league_table.rules import goal_average


def test_goal_average_is_goals_scored_divided_by_goals_conceded():
    assert goal_average(goals_for=10, goals_against=5).value == Fraction(2, 1)


def test_a_better_ratio_ranks_higher():
    assert goal_average(10, 5) > goal_average(10, 6)


def test_equal_ratios_compare_equal_even_when_the_goal_tallies_differ():
    # 2/1 and 4/2 are the same average; the raw tallies are irrelevant.
    assert goal_average(2, 1) == goal_average(4, 2)


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# The load-bearing test.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


def test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree():
    """Two clubs where the historical rule and the modern rule swap the order.

        Rovers:  10 scored,  5 conceded -> average 2.00, difference +5
        United:  25 scored, 19 conceded -> average 1.32, difference +6

    Goal AVERAGE ranks Rovers above United. Goal DIFFERENCE would rank United
    above Rovers. Level on points, 1974/75 puts Rovers first.
    """
    rovers_for, rovers_against = 10, 5
    united_for, united_against = 25, 19

    rovers = goal_average(rovers_for, rovers_against)
    united = goal_average(united_for, united_against)

    # The historical rule: Rovers ahead.
    assert rovers > united

    # The modern rule would have said the opposite. Asserting this explicitly
    # keeps the test honest -- it proves the two rules really do disagree here,
    # so the assertion above cannot pass by coincidence.
    rovers_difference = rovers_for - rovers_against
    united_difference = united_for - united_against
    assert united_difference > rovers_difference


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Edge cases. A ratio has two of them, and both are deliberate decisions.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


def test_conceding_nothing_ranks_above_every_finite_average():
    """A club that has conceded no goals divides by zero.

    The ratio is unbounded, so it outranks any finite average, however large.
    """
    unbeaten_defence = goal_average(goals_for=3, goals_against=0)

    assert unbeaten_defence > goal_average(1000, 1)
    assert unbeaten_defence.is_infinite


def test_all_infinite_averages_compare_equal():
    # 5/0 and 3/0 are both simply undefined-and-unbounded; neither is "better".
    assert goal_average(5, 0) == goal_average(3, 0)


def test_no_goals_either_way_is_undefined_and_ranks_below_every_finite_average():
    """0 scored and 0 conceded is 0/0 -- undefined, not zero.

    This is also the case for a club that has played no games at all. It cannot
    outrank a club that has actually achieved a ratio, so it sorts last.
    """
    undefined = goal_average(goals_for=0, goals_against=0)

    assert undefined.is_undefined
    assert undefined < goal_average(0, 1)  # even a club that only conceded
    assert undefined < goal_average(1, 1000)


def test_undefined_averages_compare_equal():
    assert goal_average(0, 0) == goal_average(0, 0)


def test_undefined_ranks_below_infinite():
    assert goal_average(0, 0) < goal_average(1, 0)


def test_scoring_nothing_but_conceding_is_a_real_ratio_of_zero():
    # 0/5 is a legitimate average of zero -- distinct from the 0/0 undefined
    # case above, and it still outranks undefined.
    scored_nothing = goal_average(0, 5)

    assert scored_nothing.value == Fraction(0, 1)
    assert not scored_nothing.is_undefined
    assert scored_nothing > goal_average(0, 0)


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Exactness.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


def test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding():
    """Goal average is a Fraction, not a float.

    Both of these are 0.3333333333333333 in IEEE-754 double precision, so a
    float implementation would call them equal and mis-order the clubs. Exactly,
    1/3 is the greater.
    """
    exact_third = goal_average(1, 3)
    just_under_a_third = goal_average(33_333_333_333_333_333, 100_000_000_000_000_000)

    assert float(exact_third.value) == float(just_under_a_third.value)  # floats agree
    assert exact_third > just_under_a_third  # fractions do not


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Presentation.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


def test_a_finite_average_formats_to_three_decimal_places():
    assert str(goal_average(14, 11)) == "1.273"
    assert str(goal_average(18, 6)) == "3.000"


def test_the_edge_cases_format_readably():
    assert str(goal_average(3, 0)) == "inf"
    assert str(goal_average(0, 0)) == "n/a"


def test_str_matches_the_formatted_value():
    assert str(goal_average(14, 11)) == "1.273"
    assert str(goal_average(3, 0)) == "inf"


def test_repr_is_readable():
    assert repr(goal_average(18, 6)) == "GoalAverage(3.000)"


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Behaving like a well-mannered value object.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


def test_equal_averages_hash_alike():
    # Lets averages be used in sets and as dict keys without surprises.
    assert hash(goal_average(2, 1)) == hash(goal_average(4, 2))
    assert len({goal_average(2, 1), goal_average(4, 2), goal_average(1, 2)}) == 2


def test_comparing_with_a_non_average_is_not_equal_rather_than_an_error():
    assert goal_average(2, 1) != "2.000"
    assert goal_average(2, 1) != 2


def test_ordering_against_a_non_average_is_a_type_error():
    # Refusing the comparison is safer than inventing an ordering for it.
    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
        goal_average(2, 1) < 2  # noqa: B015

attachment.snippet:
1	# League Standings — English First Division, 1974/75
2	
3	A command-line tool that reads football results from CSV and prints a league standing
4	table, using the rules in force in the English First Division in the 1974/75 season.
5	
6	The tool is general: it tabulates whatever results it is given. It knows nothing about
7	weeks, matchdays or 1974 — those live in the data, not the code.
8	
9	```
10	$ league-table --pretty data/results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv
11	Pos  Team                      P  W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
12	  1  Ipswich Town             10  8  0  2  18   6  3.000   16
13	  2  Newcastle United         10  6  2  2  19  14  1.357   14
14	  3  Manchester City          10  6  2  2  14  11  1.273   14
15	  4  Middlesbrough            10  5  3  2  15   7  2.143   13
16	  5  Liverpool                10  6  1  3  17   8  2.125   13
17	  ...
18	```
19	
20	## Setup
21	
22	Python 3.9+. No runtime dependencies; the tool itself is pure standard library.
23	
24	```bash
25	python3 -m venv .venv
26	source .venv/bin/activate
27	pip install -e .                     # installs the `league-table` command
28	pip install -r requirements-dev.txt  # pytest + coverage, for the tests
29	```
30	
31	## Usage
32	
33	Filenames, stdin/stdout, or any mixture of the two:
34	
35	```bash
36	league-table results.csv                     # file   -> stdout
37	league-table results.csv -o standings.csv    # file   -> file
38	cat results.csv | league-table               # stdin  -> stdout  (a plain unix filter)
39	league-table --pretty results.csv            # aligned text instead of CSV
40	python -m league_table results.csv           # without installing the console script
41	```
42	
43	**Input** needs four columns — `home_team`, `away_team`, `home_goals`, `away_goals` — in
44	any order. Any other column is ignored, which is how the shipped data files can carry
45	`round` and `date` for a human's benefit while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays.
46	
47	```csv
48	round,date,home_team,away_team,home_goals,away_goals
49	1,1974-08-17,Stoke City,Leeds United,3,0
50	```
51	
52	**Output** is the table in the conventional column order:
53	
54	```csv
55	Position,Team,Played,Won,Drawn,Lost,GoalsFor,GoalsAgainst,GoalAverage,Points
56	1,Ipswich Town,10,8,0,2,18,6,3.000,16
57	```
58	
59	Malformed input is rejected with a message on stderr naming the offending line. Nothing is
60	written to the output on failure — no half-written table, and an in-place rewrite
61	(`league-table results.csv -o results.csv`) is safe, because the table is rendered to memory
62	before the output is opened.
63	
64	| exit | meaning |
65	|---|---|
66	| 0 | the table was written |
67	| 2 | the input could not be read: bad CSV, missing/duplicated column, bad score, missing file |
68	| 141 | stdout was closed early (`\| head`), the conventional 128 + SIGPIPE |
69	
70	The parser rejects anything it cannot read *unambiguously* rather than guessing: a required
71	column named twice, a row with the wrong number of fields, a score that is not a plain run
72	of ASCII digits. (Python's own `int()` would accept `"1_0"` as 10 and non-ASCII digits as
73	numbers — either would silently put wrong goals in the table, which is worse than an error.)
74	Input is decoded as `utf-8-sig`, so the byte-order mark Excel and Google Sheets write by
75	default does not make the first column unreadable.
76	
77	## The rules, and why they are not the modern ones
78	
79	This is the crux of the exercise. Two rules differ from the modern game, and both were
80	verified against sources before the sort was written.
81	
82	### Two points for a win, one for a draw, none for a loss
83	
84	Three points for a win was **not** introduced in England until 1981/82. Wikipedia's
85	[1981–82 Football League][1981-82] page: *"This was the first league season with three
86	points for a win."* 1974/75 predates it, so a win is worth two.
87	
88	### Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE, not goal difference
89	
90	Goal average is a **ratio** — goals scored ÷ goals conceded. Goal difference (scored −
91	conceded) did not replace it in the Football League until 1976/77. Wikipedia's
92	[1974–75 Football League][1974-75] page:
93	
94	> Beginning with the season 1894–95, clubs finishing level on points were separated
95	> according to goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)... The goal average
96	> system was eventually scrapped beginning with the 1976–77 season.
97	
98	1974/75 sits squarely inside the goal-average era.
99	
100	**This is not a technicality — it changes the submitted table.** Middlesbrough and
101	Liverpool both finish matchday 10 on 13 points:
102	
103	| | scored | conceded | goal average | goal difference |
104	|---|---|---|---|---|
105	| Middlesbrough | 15 | 7 | **2.143** | +8 |
106	| Liverpool | 17 | 8 | 2.125 | **+9** |
107	
108	Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th. The modern rule would swap them.
109	Lower down it is starker still: Tottenham, Arsenal and QPR all end matchday 10 on 6 points
110	*and* an identical −5 goal difference — the modern rule cannot separate them at all, while
111	goal average orders them 0.706 / 0.667 / 0.615.
112	
113	Both cases are locked down by tests in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.
114	
115	### No further tie-break
116	
117	The Football League had none in 1974/75: clubs level on both points and goal average
118	genuinely shared the position. The tool falls back to alphabetical order purely so the
119	output is deterministic. That is a presentation choice, not a historical rule, and it is
120	marked as such in the code.
121	
122	## Goal average has two edge cases. Both are deliberate.
123	
124	A ratio has a denominator, so it can misbehave in ways a difference cannot:
125	
126	| case | arithmetic | decision | printed |
127	|---|---|---|---|
128	| Conceded nothing, scored something | division by zero | unbounded — ranks **above** every finite average | `inf` |
129	| Neither scored nor conceded (incl. a club with no games) | 0 ÷ 0 | undefined — ranks **below** every finite average | `n/a` |
130	
131	The reasoning: a club that has conceded nothing has an average better than any club that
132	has conceded, however many goals it scored — so it sorts top. A club with no goals either
133	way has no average at all; it has achieved nothing to be ranked *by*, so it cannot outrank
134	a club that has, and it sorts bottom. Note that 0 scored against 5 conceded is a **real**
135	average of 0.000, quite distinct from the undefined 0/0 case.
136	
137	The uncomfortable consequence, stated rather than hidden: an undefined club sorts below a
138	club with a genuine average of 0.000 — so a club that has *lost* can finish above a club
139	that is unbeaten, if they are level on points.
140	
141	```
142	Pos  Team         P  W  D  L  F  A   GAvg  Pts
143	  3  Beaten FC    2  0  1  1  0  2  0.000    1   <- lost a game
144	  4  Unbeaten FC  1  0  1  0  0  0    n/a    1   <- unbeaten, but 0/0
145	```
146	
147	Banding 0/0 with 0.000 instead does not rescue it: the two would then tie on average and
148	fall to the alphabetical tie-break, with Beaten FC still ahead. The case is genuinely
149	degenerate — it needs a goalless club, and it cannot arise in a league where every club has
150	scored or conceded at least once, as all 22 had by matchday 10 of 1974/75. We picked the
151	reading we could defend and wrote a test for it, rather than letting the sort decide by
152	accident.
153	
154	Averages are held as exact `Fraction`s, never floats — but the honest reason is narrower
155	than "floats would break it". At football scorelines they would not: a `float64` separates
156	14/11 from 25/19 with about fifteen digits to spare, and no club will ever score enough
157	goals to collide. `Fraction` is used so that the *correctness of the ordering does not
158	depend on the magnitude of the inputs at all* — the sort is exact by construction rather
159	than exact by luck, and nobody has to reason about ULPs to know the table is right. The
160	test that demonstrates the difference (`test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_

... [129 lines truncated] ...

aiTitle: Verify 1974/75 First Division rules and match data

content.text:
Everything the red team found that was mine to fix is fixed. 96 tests, 100% coverage, both real tables byte-identical.

## The one that stung

**I shipped a false claim in the README and made it the climax of the reflection.** I said worldfootball sorts its table by goal difference, "which is why Liverpool appears 7th there and 4th here — a neat illustration of why the season matters." I got that from the verification agent's summary and never opened the page. I've now read it myself: in their 13-point block Liverpool sit *last* on +9, below Middlesbrough (+8), Everton (+3) and Sheffield United (0). Goal-difference sorting would put Liverpool *first* in that block. The claim is refuted by the page it cites.

The data cross-check itself still holds — the numbers agree club by club, which is what it was for. But the story I built on top of it was wrong, and it sat inside the paragraph asserting that I check things instead of trusting the model. I've removed the claim from the README and rewritten that section of `AI_REFLECTION.md` to tell the truth: **an AI's summary of a source is not the source, and the more a claim flatters your argument, the more it deserves the click.** That's a better reflection than the one I had, and it has the advantage of being true.

## Also fixed

The `ai/` folder contained the wrong session — a 63-line prologue about where to put the project directory, with none of the actual work in it. The real 545-line transcript is now copied in. My third argument for the matchday reading was circular (real First Division tables *did* show unequal games played), so it's demoted to "a convenience, not a principle", and the README now states plainly that the matchday-10 table is a reconstruction containing an April 1975 result, while the 28 September file is the one that genuinely existed and matches 11v11 row for row. Duplicate fixtures now warn on stderr rather than silently double-count; `--version` exists; and the float test is relabelled as the in-principle argument it is rather than pretending floats would break on football scorelines.

## Two things only you can do

**1. There are no commits.** `git log` is empty; every file is untracked. The brief says to submit via your git repository, so as it stands a reviewer clones an empty repo — this alone fails the submission. Say the word and I'll commit.

**2. `AI_REFLECTION.md` is still my draft, in your voice, with my note-to-self at the top telling you to make it yours.** The red team flagged this as one of the most damaging items, and I agree: a reflection *on AI collaboration* that was outsourced to the AI and never reclaimed answers the question in the worst possible way. The material in it is real — the `__eq__` deletion, the week-10 argument you forced, the false-claim story above — but the telling should be yours. I'd rather you rewrite it than have me polish my own voice into something that reads like yours.

content: The SPAN league-table submission is built and verified: 96 tests, 100% coverage, correct 1974/75 answer, QA and red-team findings fixed. Two things need you: say the word and I'll commit (the repo has no commits yet), and rewrite AI_REFLECTION.md in your own voice. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content:
what happens if they want 10 matches, do we have all the data for until December?
Or do we move until all teams have 10 games?

content.text:
Good question, and the answer has a wrinkle worth being precise about.

**Yes — the submitted answer already has all 22 clubs on exactly 10 games.** That's what `results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv` is: 110 matches, every club P=10. So if "10 matches each" is what they want, we already have it.

The catch is *how* it gets to 10 each. It takes all matches **labelled** round 1–10, and three of those were postponed and physically played later — Leeds v Spurs (4 Dec 1974), Middlesbrough v Leicester (10 Dec 1974), Newcastle v Arsenal (23 Apr 1975). So the table reaches 10-apiece by pulling in results from the future.

**Do we have data through December?** Only those three matches. Our CSV stops at round 10; we never scraped rounds 11 onward, and don't need them for either shipped table.

Now, your second idea — *keep going until everyone has 10* — doesn't work the way it sounds, and it's worth seeing why:

The six clubs with a postponement were on 9 games after 28 Sep. They each reached 10 in round 11 on **5 Oct 1974**. But by that same date the *other sixteen* clubs had played **11**. So a date-cut at 5 Oct gives you 6 clubs on 10 and 16 on 11 — still unequal, just unequal in the other direction. There is no calendar date in 1974 on which all 22 clubs had played exactly 10 games. That's precisely why the archives resort to the round label.

There is a third construction that does give equal games with no time travel: **each club's first 10 matches actually played**. For the sixteen unaffected clubs that's rounds 1–10; for the six it's rounds 1–8, 10 and 11 (their 5 Oct game standing in for the postponed one). Everyone on 10, nothing from the future — but every club has a different cut-off date, and no archive publishes such a table, so it's a construct of our own.

| reading | all clubs P=10? | uses future results? | have the data? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rounds 1–10 *(shipped answer)* | yes | yes — 3 matches from Dec/Apr | yes |
| Played by 28 Sep 1974 *(shipped alternative)* | no — 6 clubs on 9 | no | yes |
| Each club's first 10 played | yes | no | needs round 11 (~10 min to scrape) |

My recommendation is to leave the answer as it is. "The 10th week" most naturally means ten rounds played, the rounds 1–10 table is the one worldfootball reproduces and that any reviewer can check against, and the 28 Sep table is already shipped for the other reading. The third construction is defensible but bespoke, and inventing a table nobody else publishes is a harder thing to defend in a review than adopting the standard one.

That said, it's cheap. Want me to build it as a third input/output pair?

message.content: what about another dataset that goes 10 calendar weeks into October for completeness into the ambiguity of the prompt?

content.text:
Good instinct — that's the one reading of the brief we argue against but don't actually *show*. Shipping it turns "we considered the calendar interpretation" into "here it is, judge for yourself."

The 10th calendar week is 19–25 Oct 1974 (counting from the 17 Aug kickoff), and it closes after Round 14 on Sat 19 Oct — nothing else was played before Round 15 on the 26th. So the cut is every match played on or before 19 Oct 1974, which needs rounds 11–14 that we never scraped. Clubs will land on 13 or 14 games, unequal, because of the postponements.

Scraping those now, and I'll verify the result against 11v11's published table for that exact date — a date-based table is the perfect independent check for a date-based cut.

content.text:
I now have 11v11's published table for 19 Oct 1974, and it's a gift — **it shows a `GAvg` column, and it's ordered by goal average, with an alphabetical fallback**. Look at the 15-point pair: Burnley and Newcastle are both on exactly 1.000, and Burnley is listed first. Same at 14 points, where Birmingham (1.000) precedes Wolverhampton (1.000). That's precisely the rule set we implemented — including the tie-break we chose for determinism.

So this isn't just a third dataset; it's the strongest validation target we've had. If our tool, fed every match played on or before 19 Oct 1974, reproduces all 22 rows *in 11v11's order*, that's end-to-end confirmation of the rules, the data and the sort against a published historical table.

Note the games-played column: 12 to 14, not a clean 13/14 — Leicester are on 12, having had two fixtures postponed. Waiting on the round 11–14 scrape to build it.

aiTitle: Verify 1974/75 First Division rules and match data

content.text: **Row for row, position for position, identical to 11v11's published table for 19 October 1974.** Let me verify that mechanically rather than by eye:

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
import csv
# 11v11's published table for 19 Oct 1974, transcribed from the page I read in the browser:
# Team, Pld, W, D, L, GF, GA, GAvg, Pts
published = """Liverpool 13 9 1 3 21 8 2.625 19
Manchester City 14 8 3 3 19 15 1.267 19
Ipswich Town 14 8 1 5 18 9 2.000 17
Everton 14 4 9 1 19 16 1.188 17
Middlesbrough 13 6 4 3 19 14 1.357 16
Stoke City 13 6 4 3 20 15 1.333 16
Derby County 14 5 6 3 21 19 1.105 16
Burnley 14 7 1 6 23 23 1.000 15
Newcastle United 13 5 5 3 19 19 1.000 15
West Ham United 14 5 4 5 25 22 1.136 14
Birmingham City 14 6 2 6 20 20 1.000 14
Wolverhampton Wanderers 14 4 6 4 15 15 1.000 14
Sheffield United 14 5 4 5 19 24 0.792 14
Carlisle United 14 5 3 6 12 12 1.000 13
Coventry City 13 3 6 4 18 23 0.783 12
Leeds United 13 4 3 6 16 15 1.067 11
Chelsea 13 3 5 5 13 20 0.650 11
Leicester City 12 3 4 5 16 18 0.889 10
Tottenham Hotspur 13 4 1 8 16 20 0.800 9
Queens Park Rangers 13 2 5 6 11 16 0.688 9
Luton Town 14 1 6 7 12 21 0.571 8
Arsenal 13 2 3 8 12 20 0.600 7"""

theirs = []
for line in published.splitlines():
    parts = line.rsplit(" ", 8)
    theirs.append((parts[0], *parts[1:]))

ours = []
for row in csv.DictReader(open('data/standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv')):
    ours.append((row["Team"], row["Played"], row["Won"], row["Drawn"], row["Lost"],
                 row["GoalsFor"], row["GoalsAgainst"], row["GoalAverage"], row["Points"]))

mismatches = [(i+1, t, o) for i, (t, o) in enumerate(zip(theirs, ours)) if t != o]
print(f"rows compared: {len(ours)}")
print(f"mismatches: {len(mismatches)}")
for pos, t, o in mismatches:
    print(f"  pos {pos}: 11v11={t}  ours={o}")
if not mismatches and len(ours) == 22:
    print("\nEVERY row identical to 11v11's published table -- including position order,")
    print("goal average to 3dp, and the alphabetical tie-breaks (Burnley/Newcastle 1.000;")
    print("Birmingham/Wolverhampton 1.000).")
EOF

content.text:
Zero mismatches across all 22 rows — including 11v11's own goal-average column and their position order. That's our tool reproducing a published historical table exactly.

One thing I'll be careful *not* to overclaim, having been burned once already: in the two ties (Burnley/Newcastle both 1.000, Birmingham/Wolves both 1.000) our alphabetical fallback agrees with 11v11's order — but goals-scored would order those same two pairs identically, so I can't tell from this data which rule they use. I'll say only that it agrees, not why.

Locking it in with tests:

input.command:
cat >> tests/test_1974_75_season.py <<'PYEOF'


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# The calendar reading, shipped so the ambiguity can be judged rather than
# taken on trust -- and, as it happens, the strongest validation in the repo.
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------

PLAYED_BY_19_OCT = DATA / "results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv"
STANDINGS_AS_OF_19_OCT = DATA / "standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv"


def test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly():
    """Our table for 19 October 1974, against the one 11v11 publishes.

    This is the closest thing to a ground truth available: a historical table,
    published by an archive, that prints its own goal-average column and orders
    the clubs by it. Every row below was read from
    https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/

    It exercises everything at once -- the match data, the two-points rule, the
    goal-average ratio, the ordering, and the handling of clubs on unequal games
    played (six postponed fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13).

    If any of that were wrong, this test could not pass.
    """
    published = [
        # team, P, W, D, L, GF, GA, GAvg, Pts
        ("Liverpool", "13", "9", "1", "3", "21", "8", "2.625", "19"),
        ("Manchester City", "14", "8", "3", "3", "19", "15", "1.267", "19"),
        ("Ipswich Town", "14", "8", "1", "5", "18", "9", "2.000", "17"),
        ("Everton", "14", "4", "9", "1", "19", "16", "1.188", "17"),
        ("Middlesbrough", "13", "6", "4", "3", "19", "14", "1.357", "16"),
        ("Stoke City", "13", "6", "4", "3", "20", "15", "1.333", "16"),
        ("Derby County", "14", "5", "6", "3", "21", "19", "1.105", "16"),
        ("Burnley", "14", "7", "1", "6", "23", "23", "1.000", "15"),
        ("Newcastle United", "13", "5", "5", "3", "19", "19", "1.000", "15"),
        ("West Ham United", "14", "5", "4", "5", "25", "22", "1.136", "14"),
        ("Birmingham City", "14", "6", "2", "6", "20", "20", "1.000", "14"),
        ("Wolverhampton Wanderers", "14", "4", "6", "4", "15", "15", "1.000", "14"),
        ("Sheffield United", "14", "5", "4", "5", "19", "24", "0.792", "14"),
        ("Carlisle United", "14", "5", "3", "6", "12", "12", "1.000", "13"),
        ("Coventry City", "13", "3", "6", "4", "18", "23", "0.783", "12"),
        ("Leeds United", "13", "4", "3", "6", "16", "15", "1.067", "11"),
        ("Chelsea", "13", "3", "5", "5", "13", "20", "0.650", "11"),
        ("Leicester City", "12", "3", "4", "5", "16", "18", "0.889", "10"),
        ("Tottenham Hotspur", "13", "4", "1", "8", "16", "20", "0.800", "9"),
        ("Queens Park Rangers", "13", "2", "5", "6", "11", "16", "0.688", "9"),
        ("Luton Town", "14", "1", "6", "7", "12", "21", "0.571", "8"),
        ("Arsenal", "13", "2", "3", "8", "12", "20", "0.600", "7"),
    ]

    ours = [
        (
            row["Team"], row["Played"], row["Won"], row["Drawn"], row["Lost"],
            row["GoalsFor"], row["GoalsAgainst"], row["GoalAverage"], row["Points"],
        )
        for row in table_for(PLAYED_BY_19_OCT)
    ]

    assert ours == published


def test_the_postponements_leave_clubs_on_unequal_games_by_19_october():
    # Two postponement clusters -- three round-9 fixtures and three round-13 --
    # and Leicester City were caught by both.
    table = table_for(PLAYED_BY_19_OCT)
    by_games = {row["Team"]: row["Played"] for row in table}

    assert by_games["Leicester City"] == "12"  # hit twice
    assert sorted(by_games.values()).count("13") == 10
    assert sorted(by_games.values()).count("14") == 11


def test_the_committed_calendar_week_output_is_what_the_tool_produces_today():
    produced = io.StringIO()

    assert main([str(PLAYED_BY_19_OCT)], stdout=produced) == EXIT_OK
    assert produced.getvalue() == STANDINGS_AS_OF_19_OCT.read_text()
PYEOF

3	These tests run the real 1974/75 results through the real CLI and assert on the

5	parsing or the sort, the English First Division of 1974/75 changes shape and
6	these fail.
7	"""

85	def test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference():
86	    """The rule is not academic: it moves clubs in the actual table.
87

89	
90	        Middlesbrough  15 scored,  7 conceded -> average 2.143, difference +8
91	        Liverpool      17 scored,  8 conceded -> average 2.125, difference +9
92

94	    the modern rule, and the wrong one for 1974/75 -- would swap them. This is
95	    the exercise's whole point, occurring in the submitted answer itself.

input.new_string:
### All three readings are shipped

Rather than argue the ambiguity and pick one in private, every reading is in the repo as an
input/output pair. The same binary produces all three — only the input differs, which is the
whole point of keeping the week out of the code.

| input | output | what it is |
|---|---|---|
| `results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv` (110 matches) | `standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv` | **The submitted answer.** Ten matchdays complete; all clubs P=10. |
| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv` (107 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv` | The table as it actually stood on matchday 10's evening: six clubs on P=9. |
| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv` (148 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv` | The **10th calendar week**, ending Sat 19 Oct 1974. Clubs on 12–14 games. |

The honest tension, since it cuts against the file we lead with: the matchday-10 table
contains three results that had not been played in September — one of them not until April
1975. It is a *reconstruction* of a ten-game table, not a table anyone could have printed at
the time. We lead with it because "the 10th week" most naturally means ten rounds played,
and because it is the table the archives reproduce — but a reviewer who reads the brief the
other way will find their table here too, computed by the same code.

### The calendar-week table is also the strongest evidence the tool is right

The 19 October 1974 table is worth more than completeness. [11v11 publishes the table for
that exact date][11v11-19oct], and — unusually — prints its own **goal average** column and
orders the clubs by it. Our output reproduces it **exactly: all 22 rows, in the same order,
with the same goal averages to three decimal places.**

```
Pos  Team                P   W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
  1  Liverpool           13  9  1  3  21   8  2.625   19     <- 13 games
  2  Manchester City     14  8  3  3  19  15  1.267   19     <- 14 games
  3  Ipswich Town        14  8  1  5  18   9  2.000   17
  4  Everton             14  4  9  1  19  16  1.188   17
  ...
 18  Leicester City      12  3  4  5  16  18  0.889   10     <- 12 games
```

That single comparison exercises everything at once — the match data, two points for a win,
the goal-average *ratio*, the ordering, and clubs on unequal games played (six postponed
fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13). Note Liverpool top the table on 19
points from **thirteen** games while Manchester City have 19 from **fourteen**: separated by
goal average, 2.625 to 1.267. If any part of the implementation were wrong, that table could
not come out right. It is asserted row by row in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.

(One thing we do *not* claim: in the two cases where clubs tie on both points and goal
average — Burnley/Newcastle on 1.000, Birmingham/Wolverhampton on 1.000 — our alphabetical
fallback happens to agree with 11v11's order. But goals-scored would order both pairs the
same way, so this data cannot tell us which rule 11v11 applies, and we do not pretend it
does.)

attachment.snippet:
1	# AI Reflection
2	
3	## Where the AI went wrong: it deleted code that coverage said was dead
4	
5	We worked test-first and I asked for 100% branch coverage as a gate. That combination
6	produced the most instructive failure of the session, and it was caused by the coverage
7	target itself.
8	
9	While building the sort, the coverage report flagged one uncovered line: the `__eq__`
10	method on a small private wrapper class used to sort goal averages in descending order.
11	The AI reasoned — plausibly, and out loud — that `sorted()` only ever calls `__lt__`, so
12	`__eq__` was dead code, and deleted it rather than annotate it with a `# pragma: no cover`.
13	That reasoning is wrong, and subtly so. Python compares tuples element by element, using
14	`==` to decide whether to move on to the next element. With `__eq__` gone, two clubs with
15	*identical* goal averages compared as unequal (falling back to identity), so the sort never
16	reached the third element of the key — the club name — and the alphabetical tie-break
17	became silently unreachable. Nothing would have crashed. The table would just have been
18	quietly non-deterministic for clubs level on both points and goal average.
19	
20	The test I had insisted on writing first caught it in about ten seconds, and that is the
21	whole point. Had we written the code first and the tests after, I am fairly sure the test
22	would have been written to match the behaviour rather than the rule, and the bug would have
23	shipped green. What I took from it: an uncovered line is a *question*, not a verdict. It
24	means "no test exercises this", which is exactly as consistent with "this is load-bearing
25	and untested" as with "this is dead". The AI collapsed that ambiguity in the direction that
26	made the number go up. I now treat a coverage gate as something that generates work, not
27	something that licenses deletion — and the restored `__eq__` carries a comment explaining
28	why it exists, so the next person (or model) doesn't make the same cut.
29	
30	A smaller version of the same thing happened twice more. The AI wrote a test asserting
31	Burnley would finish above Carlisle; the code disagreed, and the code was right — goal
32	average separated them and the *test* was the thing that was wrong. Writing tests first
33	does not make them correct, it just makes the disagreement surface early enough to be
34	interesting.
35	
36	## Where I pushed back: "the 10th week" is not the 10th matchday, necessarily
37	
38	The AI's first instinct — and a reasonable one — was to read "the 10th week of the season"
39	as "after 10 matchdays" and get on with it. I pushed on that, because the two readings only
40	coincide if fixtures fall one per week, and I did not believe they had. Forcing the actual
41	dates out of the data proved they had not: the League played extra midweek rounds in the
42	opening fortnight, so all 22 clubs had played 10 games by 28 September 1974, only six
43	calendar weeks in, and the literal 10th calendar week lands in *late October*, by which
44	point clubs had played thirteen or fourteen. The ambiguity was real and worth about ten
45	minutes of argument.
46	
47	I settled on the matchday reading, and the README argues it on three grounds rather than
48	asserting it. But the more useful decision was structural: since the tool is general and
49	the week lives in the data rather than the code, both cut-offs cost nothing to ship, so
50	both ship — the rounds 1–10 table as the submitted answer, and the "as it actually stood on
51	28 September" table alongside it, six clubs still on nine games because their round-9
52	fixtures were postponed to December and April. Making the tool ignorant of matchdays turned
53	a judgement call I could have got wrong into a judgement call I did not have to make
54	irreversibly.
55	
56	## What I would not let the AI skip: proving the data, not just the code
57	
58	The historical rules were verified against cited sources before the sort was written, which
59	is the part of this exercise that is actually load-bearing — a beautifully tested
60	implementation of *goal difference* would be a wrong answer, and it would be wrong silently.
61	But I also wanted the *data* proved, not just the rules. The results were scraped, and
62	scraped through a third-party text proxy at that, because the archive blocks plain HTTP
63	clients; that is transport, not provenance, and it is exactly the sort of detail that gets
64	waved through. So the computed table was cross-checked row by row against two independent
65	sources — worldfootball's own matchday-10 table and 11v11's date-based table for 28
66	September 1974 — and they agree on every club's played, won, drawn, lost, goals for, goals
67	against and points. The six clubs where 11v11 disagrees are precisely the six affected by
68	the postponements, each differing by exactly one result with no leftover goals. The two
69	datasets corroborate each other.
70	
71	And then I got caught by exactly the thing I had just congratulated myself on avoiding.
72	
73	The verification agent reported that worldfootball *displays* its table sorted by goal
74	difference, which would neatly explain why Liverpool sits 7th there and 4th in ours. It is a
75	lovely story — the historical rule, visible in the wild, on the very season the exercise
76	asks about — and I wrote it into both the README and this document without opening the page.
77	A second review checked it and it is false: in worldfootball's 13-point block, Liverpool are
78	*last* on a goal difference of +9, below Middlesbrough (+8), Everton (+3) and Sheffield
79	United (0). Goal-difference sorting would have put them first in that block. Whatever
80	explains their row order, it is not the tidy story I told, and I had cited the page that
81	disproves it.
82	
83	The data cross-check itself survived — the numbers do agree, club by club, and that is what
84	the verification was for. What did not survive was my explanation of someone else's table,
85	which I had accepted from a model summary because it was pleasing and confirmed the point I
86	wanted to make. That is the whole failure mode this exercise is about, committed by me, in
87	the paragraph claiming I don't commit it. I have removed the claim rather than patch it,
88	because I do not know what worldfootball sorts by and have no need to.
89	
90	The lesson I actually take from the session is narrower than "verify things". It is:
91	**an AI's summary of a source is not the source**, and the more a claim flatters your
92	argument, the more it deserves the click. The match data got that treatment and held up. My
93	favourite sentence in the write-up did not get it, and was wrong.
94	
95	It is also why the load-bearing test in this repo does not merely assert an ordering: it
96	asserts that goal average and goal difference *disagree* about the pair, so it cannot pass
97	by coincidence, and anyone who "modernises" the sort gets a red test rather than a plausible
98	table.

import pathlib

- **The 10th calendar week** — 19–25 October 1974, by which point clubs had played 13 or 14 games.""",
"""- **After 10 matchdays** — every club has played 10 games, reached on Saturday 28 September 1974.
- **The 10th calendar week** — 19–25 October 1974, closing after Round 14 on Saturday the 19th,
  by which point clubs had played 12 to 14 games.""")

s = s.replace("96 tests. The suite enforces", "99 tests. The suite enforces")

s = s.replace("""- `test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference` — the same
  thing, in the real season, on the real submitted table.""",
"""- `test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference` — the same
  thing, in the real season, on the real submitted table.
- `test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly` — all 22 rows of
  a **published historical table**, reproduced exactly: same order, same goal averages, clubs
  on unequal games played. The single strongest check in the repo.""")

s = s.replace("""[wf-md10]: https://www.worldfootball.net/schedule/eng-premier-league-1974-1975-spieltag/10/
[11v11]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-september-1974/""",
"""[wf-md10]: https://www.worldfootball.net/schedule/eng-premier-league-1974-1975-spieltag/10/
[11v11]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-september-1974/
[11v11-19oct]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/""")

s = s.replace("""Match results were taken from [worldfootball.net's 1974/75 fixture archive][worldfootball]
and validated on the way in: exactly 110 matches, 11 per round, each of the 22 clubs
appearing exactly once per round, and the club list an exact set-match with the 22 clubs in
Wikipedia's [final 1974–75 First Division table][1974-75].""",
"""Match results were taken from [worldfootball.net's 1974/75 fixture archive][worldfootball]
(rounds 1–14, 154 matches) and validated on the way in: 11 matches per round, each of the 22
clubs appearing exactly once per round, and the club list an exact set-match with the 22
clubs in Wikipedia's [final 1974–75 First Division table][1974-75]. Every match carries the
date it was **actually played**, not its nominal matchday — six fixtures were postponed
(three from round 9, three from round 13) and played as late as April 1975, which is what
makes the date-based cut-offs meaningful.""")
p.write_text(s)
EOF
